Title: Vibe Coding for UX Design: Understanding UX Professionals’ Perceptions of AI-Assisted Design and Development

Vibe Coding for UX Design: Understanding UX Professionals’ Perceptions of AI-Assisted Design and Development
Abstract.
Generative AI is reshaping UX design practices through “vibe coding”, where UX professionals express intent in natural language and AI translates it into functional prototypes and code. Despite rapid adoption, little research has examined how vibe coding reconfigures UX workflows and collaboration. Drawing on interviews with 20 UX professionals across enterprises, startups, and academia, we show how vibe coding follows a four-stage workflow of ideation, AI generation, debugging, and review. This accelerates iteration, supports creativity, and lowers barriers to participation. However, professionals reported challenges of code unreliability, integration, and AI over-reliance. We find tensions between efficiency-driven prototyping (“intending the right design”) and reflection (“designing the right intention”), introducing new asymmetries in trust, responsibility, and social stigma within teams. Through the lens of responsible human-AI collaboration for AI-Assisted UX design and development, we contribute a deeper understanding of deskilling, ownership and disclosure, and creativity safeguarding in the age of vibe coding.
1. Introduction
The recent proliferation of Generative AI (GenAI) tools has transformed how people design digital experiences, including how UX professionals prototype, develop, and deploy software. Built on large language and multimodal foundation models (Bommasani et al., 2021), systems such as ChatGPT111https://chat.openai.com, GitHub Copilot222https://github.com/features/copilot, Cursor333https://www.cursor.com, Lovable444https://lovable.dev, and Bolt555https://bolt.new translate natural-language prompts directly into working code, user interfaces, and interactive prototypes. Earlier AI-assisted tools, such as code autocompletion or small code snippet suggestions, were designed mainly to speed up single tasks without changing overall workflows (Svyatkovskiy et al., 2020). By contrast, these new vibe coding systems act as continuous partners: they can generate entire components, integrate APIs, and adapt outputs to user context, collapsing once-separate stages of ideation, prototyping, and implementation into a single conversational workflow. This emerging style of working with AI has been described as “vibe coding” (Karpathy, 2025), a term that entered popular discourse after Andrej Karpathy’s 2025 post on Twitter/X, describing it as “There’s a new kind of coding I call ”vibe coding”, where you fully give in to the vibes, embrace exponentials, and forget that the code even exists.” (Karpathy, 2025).
In online communities, vibe coding often refers to a prompt-to-product workflow where high-level creative intent (“the vibe”) is turned into functional artifacts with minimal setup, sometimes skipping human-led review or manual edits. Academic work has since expanded the concept: Horvat (Horvat, 2025) frames vibe coding as a paradigm where developers act as high-level coordinators, guiding AI through iterative prompts rather than writing line-by-line code; Sarkar et al. (Sarkar and Drosos, 2025) describe it as programming through dialogue; and Meske et al. (Meske et al., 2025) emphasize how it reconfigures intent mediation by redistributing epistemic labor between humans and machines. In this paper, we adopt a broad definition of vibe coding that reflects professional practice: while workflows are conversational and intent-driven, they still involve manual debugging, verification, and refinement. This broader definition distinguishes our work from narrower notions of “pure vibe coding” that are limited to quick, disposable prototypes. We frame vibe coding as relevant to professional UX workflows involving manual debugging, verification, and collaboration, even though many participants currently use it mainly for personal projects or early-stage prototyping rather than production. Despite its rapid spread, little empirical research has examined vibe coding’s implications for UX workflows, cross-functional collaboration, and organizational adoption (Subramonyam et al., 2025). Questions remain about how these tools fit into established pipelines, how they reshape human–human and human–AI collaboration, and how professional roles should be defined when the boundaries between UX designers, front-end engineers, and product managers are increasingly blurred (Li et al., 2024). To this end, we ask:
-
•
RQ1: How do UX professionals integrate vibe and AI-assisted coding into their design workflows, from ideation through prototyping to implementation?
-
•
RQ2: What opportunities and risks do UX professionals envision for human–human and human–AI collaboration in the age of vibe coding?
To address these questions, we conducted in-depth interviews with 20 professionals working across sectors and company sizes. Following prior work (Chen et al., 2025; Bruun et al., 2018), we use the term UX professionals to broadly capture roles typical of a software project team and whose daily work involves shaping user experience, including software engineers (particularly those in front-end and prototyping roles), UX/UI designers, product managers, and startup founders. This framing reflects the reality that vibe coding blurs traditional role distinctions, making UX a distributed responsibility across multiple functions.
We find that vibe coding has become a solid means for mediating between human goals and outputs, creating shifts in expertise distribution and new roles across UX practice (from prompt engineering to AI oversight), while simultaneously raising tensions between speed-driven prototyping (“intending the right design”) and deeper reflection (“designing the right intention”). Moreover, AI over-reliance in UX work not only erodes individual skill, but also introduces new asymmetries in trust, responsibility, and social stigma within teams, paradoxically widening the gap between “AI-literate” and “AI-dependent” practitioners. Furthermore, while confidence in vibe coding outputs can erode critical thinking, it can also serve as an educational opportunity; however, only for those already skilled in coding. We also find that UX professionals emphasize resisting homogenization by advocating for critical thinking, creative restraint when vibe coding, and intentionally crafting learning moments that can result in variation and artistic exploration when designing through vibe code. Lastly, in vibe coding, we find that ownership is not tied to code execution but to the act of intention-setting, creating a new distinction between authorship of ideas and executional labor. Such a reconfiguration raises questions about how to best disclose AI usage, ownership and authorship for code and design, and accountability in UX teams. We discuss the implications of vibe coding, within the lens of ensuring responsible human-human and human-AI collaboration within AI-Assisted UX design and development practices.
2. Background and Related Work
We first review generative AI tools for UX design, which embed AI into design and development workflows. Second, we discuss research on how generative AI reconfigures UX workflows and roles, situating this work within broader studies of AI in creative and professional domains. Third, we turn to vibe coding as an emerging authoring paradigm, highlighting how our study extends prior work by bringing the concept into the context of UX practice.
2.1. Generative AI Powered Tools for UX Design
HCI research has long examined how to support UI and UX design through new tools (Kort et al., 2007; Buxton, 2010; Frich et al., 2019). As generative AI has entered practice, researchers have begun embedding it directly into design workflows (Kaiser, 2019; Stige et al., 2023; Wan and Lu, 2023; Han and Cai, 2023). Evaluations consistently show AI can augment human creativity by providing novel perspectives (Chiou et al., 2023) and enabling two-way exchanges of ideas between designers and systems (Lawton et al., 2023).
A wide range of systems illustrate these directions. Prompt-to-UI tools such as WireGen and PromptInfuser generate mid-fidelity wireframes or bind prompts to Figma elements, improving speed and iteration (Feng et al., 2023b; Li et al., 2024). Co-ideation systems like Cobbie, DesignWeaver, and UIDEC scaffold sketching, text-to-image exploration, and constraint-driven ideation, helping designers diversify ideas while staying within workflow constraints (Lin et al., 2020; Kulkarni et al., 2023; Bunian et al., 2021; Tao et al., 2025; Shokrizadeh et al., 2025). Exploration and search tools include VINS, which retrieves inspirations from sketches (Bunian et al., 2021), Park’s multimodal LLM for semantic search on mobile UIs (Park et al., 2025), and Lu’s Flowy and Misty, which surface abstract design patterns and blend design examples (Lu et al., 2024, 2025). Studies of brainstorming systems like Xu et al.’s reflective design framework (Xu et al., 2025) and Wang et al.’s AIdeation platform (Wang et al., 2025) show that AI support yields more diverse concepts, higher satisfaction, and efficiency, though it may shift effort toward comprehension and synthesis.
Other work extends AI into integrated environments. Zamfirescu-Pereira et al. (Zamfirescu-Pereira et al., 2025) present Pail, an IDE with multiple LLM agents for exploring problem framings, decisions, and assumptions, broadening participants’ design search space. Generative AI has also been applied to user research: Shin et al. (Shin et al., 2024) used LLMs to generate and simulate personas to aid empathy, while Duan et al. (Duan et al., 2024) developed GPT-4-based feedback for UI mockups, finding value in catching errors and text issues though diminishing utility across iterations.
Across these systems, prior work maps the design space of AI-powered creativity support (Ning et al., 2023), showing they augment workflows but also introduce new responsibilities such as debugging, error management, and bias mitigation (Li et al., 2024; Danry et al., 2023). Some approaches treat prompts themselves as reusable design material to support iteration (Li et al., 2024). Alongside opportunities, scholars also highlight risks. Chat and Wong (Cha and Wong, 2025) document factors driving non-use among UX professionals, including the collaborative nature of UX, professional responsibility, and organizational constraints. Shin et al. (Shin et al., 2025) show that LLM-assisted reframing can exacerbate disparities between novices and experts and undermine novices’ sense of agency.
Together, this body of work demonstrates the potential of generative AI to accelerate ideation, prototyping, and evaluation, while also surfacing challenges around professional agency, accountability, and equitable skill development. Yet these systems remain largely research prototypes evaluated in specific settings, and have not been adopted or popularized at scale. Our work builds on this foundation by examining how UX practitioners adopt and integrate vibe coding—enabled through popular products such as Copilot and Cursor—into their everyday workflows.
2.2. Generative AI’s Impact on UX Workflows, Roles, and Collaboration
Generative AI has demonstrated wide-ranging effects on creative and knowledge work. Large language models could affect at least 10% of tasks for 80% of the U.S. workforce, and over half of tasks for nearly one-fifth (Eloundou et al., 2023). Analyses show widespread adoption of generative AI across writing, software development, and other economically valuable tasks (Handa et al., 2025; Tomlinson et al., 2025; Liang et al., 2025a, b). Empirical studies demonstrate measurable productivity effects: LLMs reduce task completion time in writing and communication (Peng et al., 2023; Noy and Zhang, 2023), improve call center performance especially for lower-skilled workers (Brynjolfsson et al., 2025b), and boost consultant performance in field settings (Dell’Acqua et al., 2023, 2025). At the same time, impacts vary: enabling advanced AI tools can slow skilled developers (Becker et al., 2025), workers benefit differently depending on their experience profile (Wang et al., 2023a), and long-term creativity may be diminished despite short-term gains (Kumar et al., 2025). Non-experts also struggle to design effective prompts, often treating LLMs as human interlocutors rather than computational tools (Zamfirescu-Pereira et al., 2023). Beyond productivity, generative AI raises concerns about bias, stereotyping, privacy, environmental cost, and hidden moderation labor (Solaiman et al., 2023), as well as risks of deskilling (Eloundou et al., 2023; Li et al., 2024). Scholars emphasize the importance of keeping humans “in the loop” to maintain expertise, accountability, and meaningful engagement in AI-augmented work (Amershi et al., 2019; Shi et al., 2023).
These broader dynamics are mirrored in UX design. Tholander et al. (Tholander and Jonsson, 2023) show how generative models support early ideation and sketching, while Feng et al. (Feng et al., 2023a) illustrate how practitioners treat AI outputs as design material. Designers increasingly use AI to generate alternatives, edge-case probes, and copy suggestions, expanding the space of possible designs (Yan et al., 2022; Tholander and Jonsson, 2023). Interviews with professional UI/UX designers find they value AI as a creative partner during divergent stages, while still emphasizing control and alignment with established practices (Khan et al., 2025). Yet perceptions differ by experience level: Li et al. (Li et al., 2024) report that senior designers see AI as assistive, while junior designers worry about creativity exhaustion, skill loss, and job replacement. Wang et al. (Wang et al., 2023b) identify practices for responsible AI in UX, including building an RAI lens, responsible prototyping, and evaluation.
Generative AI also reshapes collaboration and role boundaries. Designers now prototype through code, while developers generate copy or layout ideas, blurring traditional divisions of labor (Yildirim et al., 2022; Ritala et al., 2023). This flexibility raises ambiguity around authorship, ownership, and disclosure (Constantinides et al., 2025; El Ali et al., 2024). Draxler et al. (Draxler et al., 2024) find users often self-attribute AI outputs, while Clark et al. (Clark et al., 2024) highlight tensions between disclosure and originality. Teams adopt new governance strategies such as rotating “AI editor” roles, shared prompting conventions, and lightweight mechanisms to track model use (Shi et al., 2023; Liu et al., 2023; Danry et al., 2023), especially in light of disclosure requirements (El Ali et al., 2024). Trust further shapes these dynamics: cooperative AI behavior fosters collaboration (Martelaro et al., 2022), while autonomous or biased outputs can propagate human error and break norms (Danry et al., 2023; Vicente and Matute, 2023). These shifts create demand for new competencies, including prompt writing, debugging, and critical interpretation (Ritala et al., 2023; Eloundou et al., 2023).
Finally, research highlights collaboration challenges in cross-functional teams developing AI systems. Hari et al. (Subramonyam et al., 2025) find prompt-guided prototyping broadens participation across roles, while Bruun et al. (Bruun et al., 2025) document risks of compartmentalization, asymmetric power, and weak cross-disciplinarity, recommending tighter integration between UX and AI teams.
Together, this literature shows that while generative AI expands creative capacity and reconfigures collaboration, it also raises challenges for authorship, accountability, trust, and governance. Our study builds on this work by examining how vibe coding, as a new paradigm of AI-assisted development, specifically reshapes workflows and collaboration for UX professionals.
2.3. Vibe Coding as a New Form of AI-Assisted Authoring
Vibe coding captures a shift from manual artifact creation toward intent-driven collaboration with generative AI systems. Popularized after Karpathy’s 2025 post on X, the term describes practices where tools such as Cursor, GitHub Copilot, Lovable, and Bolt generate and refine artifacts through natural language dialogue. Sarkar and Drosos (Sarkar and Drosos, 2025) define vibe coding as a workflow of goal setting, prompting, evaluation, and iterative refinement, while Meske (Meske et al., 2025) frame it as a collaborative flow in which human intent is mediated probabilistically rather than deterministically. Unlike earlier generative AI tools that focused on discrete tasks or outputs, vibe coding treats AI as a continuous authoring partner across the entire making process, blurring boundaries between ideation, prototyping, and implementation.
HCI and software engineering research has begun to document how these practices reshape programming workflows. Tools such as Codex and Copilot accelerate production but reallocate effort from authoring to validation and debugging, often characterized as a “generate–test–edit” cycle (Kazemitabaar et al., 2023; Liu et al., 2023). Ross et al. (Ross et al., 2023) demonstrate how conversational assistants support not only code generation but also explanation and iterative exploration, extending interaction models beyond direct manipulation and search. Large-scale surveys reveal a complex balance of benefits and concerns: programmers value efficiency and clarity but report challenges with inaccuracies, limited contextual awareness, and over-reliance, alongside frustrations when outputs fail to meet functional requirements or resist steering (Akhoroz and Yildirim, 2025; Sergeyuk et al., 2025; Liang et al., 2024).
Despite these insights, most research focuses narrowly on professional developers. As vibe coding practices diffuse into adjacent domains—including product management and UX design—open questions remain about how AI-assisted authoring alters workflows, blurs professional boundaries, and reshapes design practice. Our study addresses this gap by examining vibe coding in the context of UX professionals. In doing so, we extend prior work beyond programming to show how vibe coding, as a new form of AI-assisted authoring, is being adopted through popular products and reconfigures the practices of design and collaboration.
3. Method
We employed a semi-structured interview methodology for this study (Longhurst, 2003) to investigate how professionals across design, engineering, and product roles integrate AI coding agents into their UI design and frontend implementation workflows. Our aim was to capture their goals, workflows, challenges, and perceptions of these tools’ value in practice.
3.1. Participants and Interview Procedure
Our target group included UX professionals involved in UX design, frontend and backend development, product management, UX research, and hybrid designer-developer roles, reflecting the interdisciplinary nature of vibe coding practices.
Recruitment. We distributed a screening questionnaire across online communities and networks frequented by these professionals, including LinkedIn, Twitter/X, Discord servers for AI-assisted development, and mailing lists for AI design tool communities. The questionnaire captured professional role, years of experience, organizational context, and prior use of AI coding agents (e.g., Cursor, Replit, Bolt, V0, Lovable) for generating or refining UI artifacts. Eligibility required hands-on experience with at least one such tool for professional work or substantial side projects. We received 48 responses and selected 20 participants to ensure diversity across roles, organizational sizes, regions, and use cases. This sample size aligns with prior HCI research on thematic saturation, which typically occurs around 15 participants (M = 15, SD = 6) (Caine, 2016; Hennink and Kaiser, 2022).

Interviews. Each participant completed a 60-minute online interview between May and June 2025. One researcher conducted the interview while another took notes. With participant consent, all sessions were audio-recorded and transcribed. The protocol included open-ended questions on: (1) background and current workflows; (2) experiences and challenges with vibe coding tools; (3) collaboration dynamics within teams; and (4) perceived value, limitations, and future expectations. The full interview guide is provided in Supplementary Material A, and flexibility was maintained to probe emergent topics.
Participants. To ensure anonymity while maintaining traceability, participants were labeled P1–P20. The sample comprised 9 females and 11 males from North America (n = 12), Europe (n = 6), and Asia (n = 2). Professional experience ranged from less than 3 years (n = 5) to over 5 years (n = 9). Organizational sizes ranged from large enterprises (10,000 employees; n = 8), to large organizations (1,000–10,000; n = 4), medium organizations (500–1,000; n = 2), small startups (50; n = 3), and academic institutions (n = 3) (Table 2). All participants had direct experience with vibe coding tools, most commonly Cursor (n = 16), Replit (n = 11), Lovable (n = 9), Bolt (n = 8), and V0 (n = 8). Many combined tools in multi-step workflows. Table 1 summarizes participant roles, example tasks, and typical tools.

3.2. Data Analysis
We used Miro 666Miro: https://miro.com for remote collaboration on data analysis. The audio recordings of the interviews were transcribed. We analyzed the data following a deductive/inductive hybrid thematic analysis approach (Fereday and Muir-Cochrane, 2006; Xu and Zammit, 2020). First, five researchers coded the transcripts deductively according to six categories in our code manual (see Supplementary Material B), developed from the research questions and interview guide. With participant consent, all coded data were turned into 565 digital statement cards on Miro, each containing a participant ID (P1–P20), a direct quote, and a one-sentence summary. Instead of calculating inter-rater reliability, we reached consensus through daily meetings and workshops (McDonald et al., 2019).
Because the initial categories were broad, we then applied an inductive approach to cluster the statement cards using affinity diagramming (Harboe and Huang, 2015a), consolidating them into sub-themes and themes. This process resulted in six final themes. To ensure trustworthiness (cf., (Nowell et al., 2017)), we describe the full five-step hybrid analysis process in detail in Supplementary Material B. All procedures adhered to data protection and privacy (incl. GDPR) requirements and the ethical research policy of the last author’s institution.
4. Findings
Our analysis revealed six interrelated themes. Theme 1: Vibe coding workflows encompassed a four-stage process of ideation, AI generation, manual debugging, and testing, together with best practices and contrasts to Figma. Theme 2: Benefits included productivity, cognitive offloading, learning, and creativity. Theme 3: Challenges covered unreliability, difficulties in fine-tuning and debugging, pseudo productivity, over reliance, limited context, and integration barriers. Theme 4: Trust and accountability addressed shifting AI trust, security and privacy concerns, and debates on authorship. Theme 5: Collaboration described AI driven role shifts, tensions between junior and senior roles, AI as a team member, and organizational barriers. Theme 6: Future developments focused on skills to learn and unlearn and visions of workflows with proactive domain specialized agents and democratized, autonomous product development.
4.1. Theme 1: Four Stages in a Vibe Coding Workflow for UX Design
Theme 1 outlines a four-stage workflow that blends creative planning, AI-supported generation, and human refinement to accelerate prototyping while maintaining quality. Although structured sequentially, the process is highly iterative: Testing and Review (Stage 4) often leads back to Debugging (Stage 3) or AI Generation (Stage 2), while debugging may expose missing context that returns work to Ideation (Stage 1). This back-and-forth continues until both functional reliability and design intent are achieved (Fig. 1).
4.1.1. Theme 1.1: Best Practices for Each Stage in the Vibe Coding UX Design Workflow
Stage 1: Context Setup and Ideation. Participants began by sketching architectures, defining goals, or identifying pain points before involving AI tools. They also gathered visual or textual references, such as UI screenshots, earlier design versions, or Figma snippets, to provide input for vibe coding. General-purpose LLMs like ChatGPT, Gemini, or Claude were often used for brainstorming, exploring alternative directions, or background research.
Best practices for Stage 1 focused on clear and precise communication, as P17 explained: “You really have to master how you communicate with the AI. Everything is language-based, and if you’re not precise enough with your words or the way you phrase your prompts, you basically lose control over what it does.”[P17] P14 illustrated this metaphorically with the “glass of water” example: “If I just say ‘create me a glass of water,’ it might make a model of that shape which could be glass, or bricks, or wood, too generic to be useful. Instead, you need to break it down: first the water, then how much water, then the glass, then the shape, and finally put them together.”[P14] P19 noted that this precision, while time-consuming, was ultimately valuable: “It slows you down at the micro level… But at the same time, it makes you sharper and forces you to think and communicate better.”[P19] Others stressed the importance of providing rich situational context, as P16 described: “It’s helpful if you first ask AI to learn from the historical context… it’s basically a training process.”[P16] P18 echoed: “We need to provide as much detail as possible so the model understands our purpose and situation.”[P18] Some participants used LLMs as strategic planners, treating them as tools for structuring requirements. As P6 noted: “I just feed the whole problem into the model, and it comes back with a table of strategies ranked by intrusiveness.”[P6] Finally, many highlighted the importance of balancing AI patterns with human creativity. As P17 explained: “AI’s just working off patterns it’s already seen, so I always set aside time to brainstorm on my own. That way I’m still coming up with fresh ideas, and the AI’s more like my copilot than the one driving.”[P17]
Stage 2: AI Generation and Refinement. With goals and references in place, participants engaged specialized vibe coding tools to produce UI layouts, components, or code. Tools like Cursor, Replit, Bolt, V0, and Lovable served distinct roles, ranging from Cursor’s inline debugging to Replit’s collaborative hosting, Bolt’s instant UI previews, V0’s rapid page scaffolding, and Lovable’s text-to-app iteration with backend logic (Table 3). Once outputs were generated, participants refined them through prompt adjustments or updated visual references, engaging in an iterative back-and-forth until the results aligned with expectations.
| Tool | Description | Illustrative Quote |
| Cursor | Praised for inline debugging and context-aware rewrites, enabling developers to refine code without leaving the editor. | P12: “Cursor is great because it allows me to debug and tweak code inside the editor without switching context.” |
| Replit | Supported collaborative experimentation and fast testing with built-in hosting. | P9: “Replit is better when I need to work collaboratively or test something quickly online.” |
| Bolt | Provided immediate visual previews from natural language or Figma designs, supporting rapid UI iteration. | P5: “I use Bolt when I want more visual feedback and test different design ideas. It gives me instant UI previews. It feels like working with a visual playground.” |

Best practices for Stage 2 centered on effective prompting strategies. Participants stressed the importance of well-structured prompts broken into step-by-step tasks, as P12 explained: “I break things down into small, single-purpose steps so the AI can really focus. If I just throw in a generic prompt without clear positioning or logic, the results are awful. It’s either messy, incomplete, or totally off from what I had in mind.”[P12] Another recurring theme was the need to refine outputs in small increments rather than making large changes at once. As P11 noted: “Generate a small code at a time; if it fails, revert to the previous step. If I change it too much at once, it might hallucinate or just forget part of the request.”[P11] Finally, participants highlighted the value of providing concrete examples and targeted design changes—such as highlighting specific code segments, chatting directly with the AI, or supplying screenshots—to guide refinement more effectively. As P13 described: “When I need to resize a button, I use targeted prompts by highlighting the code, chatting directly with the AI, or sending a screenshot to show exactly what I want changed.”[P13]
Stage 3: Manual Debugging and Editing. Even after refinement, AI-assisted code still required substantial human intervention. Participants frequently fixed callbacks, adjusted backend logic, and ensured production-quality standards, especially when AI outputs failed in edge cases.
Best practices for Stage 3 underscored that AI-generated outputs still required human judgment to ensure quality and correctness. As P17 noted: “Sometimes Cursor gives you lines of code that don’t do anything at all. They’re there, but they have no effect, just redundant. That’s why you always need someone on the team who really knows what’s going on in the codebase, to catch these things. Otherwise, there’s basically no firewall to stop those useless or even harmful lines from slipping through into production.”[P17] A manual review before deployment was widely seen as mandatory, as P3 stressed: “All the code has to be reviewed manually before deployment. I never just take what the AI gives me.”[P3] Participants also described stepping in when AI outputs were brittle or failed in edge cases. As P5 explained: “If AI fails, I step in and understand the code before fixing it. This is especially true for edge cases where AI output is brittle.”[P5] Finally, some used AI as a first pass to identify issues, but always verified results themselves. As P7 described: “Sometimes I just get lazy and type ‘error’ to let Cursor debug for me. It’s a quick way to see what Cursor thinks is wrong, but I never trust it blindly. I still go in and check the fix myself.”[P7]
Stage 4: Testing and Review. The final stage focused on evaluating prototypes for functionality, usability, and aesthetics. Participants tested interactive behaviors, verified business logic, and assessed visual coherence, while also deciding whether outputs were ready for large-scale deployment.
Best practices for Stage 4 highlighted that testing went beyond checking visual appeal to include verifying functional correctness and assessing production readiness. As P11 explained: “I test UI iterations in real time, clicking around to see how they behave.”[P11] P14 also underscored the importance of interactive testing. Others stressed that components must be evaluated for both behavior and appearance, as P3 noted: “I check whether the component behaves as expected, not just whether it looks right.”[P3] Participants further pointed to the need for critically assessing production readiness. As P15 cautioned: “I can see the UI right there and judge if it looks good. But putting it out for a big audience? No way. It hasn’t been through any real security checks or code reviews, so there’s nothing stopping bugs or even serious issues from slipping through.”[P15] Finally, issues uncovered during testing often fed back into refinement or debugging stages, creating iterative loops until both technical and aesthetic standards were satisfied. As P2 described: “When I find an issue during testing, I either adjust the prompt to fix it or switch to manual debugging before re-testing.”[P2]
4.1.2. Theme 1.2: Workflow Changes Compared to Non-AI Tools
Vibe coding largely shifts how participants approach product and software development, particularly in replacing or streamlining traditional, non-AI-driven design processes (Table 4). At the time of our study (prior to Figma’s AI product launch in July 2025777Figma officially launched its AI feature, Make, on July 24, 2025.), participants compared vibe coding primarily against conventional tools such as Figma, Sketch, or Adobe XD. For some, AI-assisted workflows eliminated the need for pixel-perfect mockups, allowing them to move directly into interactive prototyping and test ideas more quickly. “I don’t really need to design things in Figma anymore. With these AI tools, I can jump straight to creating interactive prototypes and plug them into the codebase almost instantly.”[P1]
Alongside speed, priorities also shifted. Whereas traditional UX workflows often began with polished visuals, vibe coding encouraged starting from abstract concepts and focusing on functionality first. “That’s fine… because at this stage you just want to test the idea and see if the flow works. The exact visual details don’t matter yet. Aesthetics aren’t the priority. I just want to make sure the functionality is there.”[P8]
Collaboration dynamics also changed. In conventional workflows, sketches or wireframes moved through multiple rounds of feedback, which participants described as slow and fragmented. “In Figma, you get overloaded with comments from designers, developers, everyone leaves notes.”[P15] In contrast, vibe coding enabled individuals to quickly produce and adjust working prototypes without heavy back-and-forth. Still, participants emphasized that Figma remains the tool of choice for large, production-level projects requiring pixel-perfect visuals and clear communication across stakeholders. Vibe coding was seen as most effective for personal or early-stage projects, where speed, experimentation, and functional prototyping take precedence. “I don’t use Figma if I’m by myself. For me, Figma is more of a communication tool.”[P12] “Cursor works best for producing ready-to-development prototypes in my personal projects, but I don’t really use it as much in team-based settings.”[P5]
