4.2. Theme 2: Vibe Coding Benefits
Theme 2 shows how vibe coding boosts productivity and creativity by speeding up prototyping, reducing mental workload, and freeing time for higher-level design and decision-making.
4.2.1. Theme 2.1: Positive Impact on Productivity, Learning, and Cognitive Offloading
Vibe coding tools were widely seen as boosting productivity, enabling rapid prototyping, supporting learning, and reducing cognitive load. Participants emphasized that AI accelerates the transition from abstract concepts to working prototypes, allowing them to skip time-consuming pixel-perfect mockups and focus on testing ideas early. “From ideas in my head to ideas I can see. It’s like having a thought partner to explore, learn, and debug with and I can get feedback almost instantly.”[P9] “What used to take me hours, I can now do in two minutes with AI.”[P3]
Beyond speed, participants highlighted cognitive offloading as a major benefit. AI tools can track and recall details across large projects, reducing the mental effort required to manage complex codebases. This also allowed for smoother context switching and faster resumption of work after interruptions. “Our brains can’t remember every detail of the code, especially when there are many files or complex structures. But AI can keep track of all the details and recall them instantly. This helps a lot with repeated tasks and saves time because we don’t have to look everything up or try to remember every small part.”[P18]
Vibe coding also increased independence and autonomy, particularly for designers and less-technical team members. By directly generating functional prototypes, they could bypass reliance on developers for basic builds. “We don’t need to rely on developers anymore just to build simple prototypes. The process is much faster and more in our hands.”[P19] AI-assisted debugging streamlined workflows by catching issues earlier. “I let Cursor debug for me. It’s not perfect, but it saves time.”[P7]
For junior practitioners, these tools supported onboarding and skill development, enabling them to learn by exploring and iterating directly within the codebase. “With agent mode, I can navigate unfamiliar codebases much faster and focus on building features instead of spending days just figuring out where everything is.”[P20]
Finally, participants emphasized that by automating tedious or repetitive tasks, vibe coding tools freed them to focus on higher-level validation and decision-making. This included activities such as competitor analysis and pattern discovery. “Vibe coding helps me cut down on tedious manual work. When I’m ideating, I might turn to ChatGPT or Figma Plugins to see examples of how competitors approach similar design challenges. AI speeds up this competitor analysis so I can spend more time on creative refinement.”[P10]
4.2.2. Theme 2.2: Positive Impact on Creativity
Participants described vibe coding tools as powerful enablers of creativity, particularly by lowering the barriers to getting started and unblocking the early stages of idea generation. Rather than facing a blank screen or an empty whiteboard, these tools provided an immediate starting point that participants could refine. “It helps reduce friction in the early design process. I can easily overcome the whiteboard fear. I just input inspirations and build upon them.”[P13]
Another creative advantage was the ability to generate and validate diverse concepts in parallel. Participants noted that AI enabled exploration of multiple directions quickly, complementing human creativity. “AI follows first principles and generates ideas through probabilistic distributions. It finds connections and problems from seemingly unordered things. Compared to human creativity, which is more about convergence thinking, AI works differently — it has access to larger datasets and greater brain capacity.”[P6]
Finally, participants noted that vibe coding offered valuable mental space for reflection and inspiration. Even the pauses during code generation gave room for creative reset. “On busy days, I don’t get the chance to draw or build things. There’s no room to breathe. But when I use vibe coding tools and I’m waiting for the results to generate, that pause actually gives me a moment to breathe and reset. That space helps me be creative.”[P14]
4.3. Theme 3: Vibe Coding Challenges
While vibe coding offers productivity and creativity benefits, participants also identified challenges that limit its reliability, precision, and integration into production workflows. These challenges span technical limitations, workflow disruptions, and broader concerns about over-reliance and creative depth.
4.3.1. Theme 3.1: Unreliability, Redundancy, and Integration Challenges
Participants emphasized that vibe coding is unsuitable for production-level tasks, particularly when projects involve complex back-end systems, security-sensitive operations, or large-scale deployments. While AI tools perform well for rapid front-end prototyping, they often slow down iteration after reaching the Minimum Viable Product (MVP) stage, especially for experienced coders seeking fine-grained customization. “It works fine for quick UI ideas, but backend work or anything complex just doesn’t hold up. Developers are still needed for security, optimization, and scaling.”[P8]
Key limitations included difficulties meeting exact UI specifications, generating cross-platform-compatible designs, and producing flexible, dynamic code instead of hard-coded solutions. Backend integration was also cited as unreliable, with failures to connect Application Programming Interfaces (APIs) even when valid tokens were provided. “I couldn’t get it to connect to the OpenAI API, even with the right key.”[P11]
Participants also raised concerns about system-level integration and sharing. Database connections and tool interoperability were particularly challenging, with conflicting suggestions from different AI models creating confusion. “Plugging AI code into my system is tricky. Databases are the worst. The UI might look fine, but the database connection almost always breaks with wrong queries. I end up fixing it manually. And when I use different tools like Claude in Cursor and GitHub Copilot, they don’t talk to each other — one undoes or misreads what the other did, and I’m stuck deciding which to trust.”[P1] Sharing prototypes also proved unreliable; while local builds worked, cloud deployment was often fragile, hindering collaboration. “It works locally, but cloud deployment just keeps breaking.”[P9]
Another reliability concern was AI’s overconfidence and tendency to hallucinate, producing incorrect or irrelevant code with unwarranted certainty. Non-coders risked missing these errors altogether. “If you can’t read code well, you may not even notice when it’s wrong.”[P8]
AI codebases also tended to become bloated over time, generating redundant or unnecessary code that slowed performance and caused context loss. Large projects exceeding 5,000 lines were particularly prone to these issues. “It can balloon into something unmanageable. The AI just keeps adding stuff, and before you know it, the code’s bloated and a pain to work with.”[P13]
Finally, compared to traditional design tools like Figma, AI-generated layouts lacked pixel-level fidelity and adaptability across screen sizes, requiring manual correction. “Designers make more contextual decisions for mobile versus desktop that AI just doesn’t capture.”[P12]
4.3.2. Theme 3.2: Difficulty in Fine-Tuning, Debugging, and Version Control
Participants described persistent difficulties in making small, precise changes to AI-generated code. Even minor UI adjustments could cause breakages, trigger hallucinations, or introduce inconsistencies. This fragility often slowed workflows rather than accelerating them, especially for tasks requiring deployment or exact responsiveness. “It struggles with exact positioning or maintaining responsiveness across devices. You have to be extremely specific in your prompts.”[P12]
Debugging complex behaviors, such as front-end interactions or database queries, was especially time-consuming. AI agents sometimes became stuck in repetitive correction loops, endlessly modifying the same element without resolving the issue, or drifting away from the original design intent. “It forgets what the outcome was supposed to be, and I have to keep pulling it back on track.”[P15]
AI debugging processes often lacked attribution and tracking, making it difficult for participants to identify the source of problems or trace changes across iterations. “Debugging is difficult and opaque. You can’t always tell if the problem came from my prompt, the AI’s own logic, or some earlier change it made to the code.”[P10]
A further barrier was the lack of robust version control. While some tools like GitHub and Lovable had versioning features, others required manual workarounds such as copying AI-generated code into temporary documents for iteration. Participants also criticized the absence of transparent, human-readable histories of AI actions, making it difficult to trace errors or understand why certain decisions were made. “We need detailed histories of AI actions to improve reliability and responsibility.”[P17]
Finally, participants observed that AI-generated outputs were often prone to over-delivery, producing unintended features such as extra UI components or multi-page layouts without orchestration. Poor code management, excessive file creation, and lack of control over changes further complicated collaboration. “Sometimes it just spits out way too much, giving random components I never asked for, and then I have to clean it all up.”[P17]
4.3.3. Theme 3.3 Pseudo-Productivity, Shallow Creativity, and Over-Reliance
Some participants warned of a “productivity paradox”, where speed gains encouraged taking on more work, increasing pressure without necessarily improving outcomes. “I get more projects, they try to beat me more, although it gives me pleasure to see my potential.”[P19]
Others noted that AI’s convenience could lead to shallow creativity, with teams settling for first-draft outputs rather than exploring deeper, more original solutions. “It’s easy to stop at the easy answer instead of pushing for depth. You get something decent from the AI, and it feels good enough to move on. But that can make you skip the harder, messier exploration where the really creative breakthroughs often happen.”[P17]
Concerns about over-reliance were also common. While vibe coding reduced dependency on developers, it risked replacing this with dependency on AI, potentially eroding foundational coding and design skills, particularly for new practitioners. Participants stressed the need for intentional breaks, human review, and diversity in design approaches to avoid homogenization and complacency. “I’m definitely more independent from developers now, but I do rely on AI a lot. My worry is that for newer people, they might skip learning the basics and just trust whatever the AI gives them. If we’re not careful, everything will start looking and working the same. I think it’s important to step back sometimes, get human feedback, and keep different design voices alive.”[P14]
4.3.4. Theme 3.4 Lack of Contextual Understanding and Memory
AI’s limited contextual awareness and memory were seen as critical technical limitations. Tools often forgot prior instructions within the same session or failed to maintain project context across sessions, leading to repeated explanations and rework. Domain-specific knowledge, such as product-specific components, was also lacking, making it harder for AI to interpret or fix errors accurately. “It forgets things really quickly. Sometimes even in the same session I have to repeat myself, and across sessions it has no idea what we talked about before. Once the project gets big, it loses track of the context and slows down. It also doesn’t really know our product-specific rules, so it makes mistakes that a human familiar with the system wouldn’t.”[P6]
Multimodal limitations further constrained workflows, as AI struggled to interpret images beyond simple screenshots and could not handle audio or video-based feedback effectively. Large, complex codebases exacerbated these issues, with context loss leading to slower performance and increased manual intervention. “It’s fine if I just send a screenshot, but anything beyond that, like audio or video, it can’t really handle. And once the codebase gets big, it starts forgetting things and losing context.”[P17]
4.4. Theme 4: AI Trust, Ownership, and Accountability
Participants’ trust in AI tools for UX and product development was situational, shaped by task complexity, project maturity, and domain-specific stakes. AI was valued for low-risk, early-stage, and well-defined tasks, but rarely trusted for production systems or high-stakes decision-making. Alongside technical trust, participants reflected on shifting notions of ownership, authorship, and accountability in AI-augmented work.
4.4.1. Theme 4.1 AI Trust Boundaries and Fit for Use
AI tools were most trusted for brainstorming, ideation, and early-stage prototyping, particularly for simple UI tasks, layout generation, and visual rendering where architectural soundness was less critical. These scenarios often involved work that followed established and well-documented patterns, such as common UI layouts or standard coding structures, and low-stakes personal projects. “Simple UI tasks, generating early-stage prototypes. That’s where it shines.”[P10]
Trust declined sharply in production contexts, especially for backend integration (e.g., setting up database connections, authentication systems, or API gateways), performance-critical systems (e.g., real-time analytics dashboards or high-traffic e-commerce platforms), and complex business logic (e.g., financial transaction validation, medical image processing, or multi-step workflow automation). Multi-stakeholder workflows and backend functionalities such as databases or APIs were also seen as poor fits, particularly when coordination across designers, developers, and Quality Assurance (QA) teams was required. “I just don’t trust AI when it comes to integrating with production. It’s fine for prototypes or quick UI work, but once you’re dealing with live systems, real data, large audience, it can break in ways you don’t see coming.”[P1]
4.4.2. Theme 4.2 Evolving Trust
Some participants described a gradual increase in trust toward vibe coding AI tools, noting that while skepticism remained, especially for production-level projects, they had seen tangible improvements in reliability and usability over time. Early frustrations often came from high error rates, hallucinations, and outputs that felt too generic to be useful. As participants put it, updates that introduced clearer error detection and feedback loops changed their perception of what AI could reliably handle. “I trust AI more for small personal tasks now. I’ve seen hallucination issues improve, and I like how Cursor sometimes flags potential mistakes in its output. It’s not perfect, but that kind of feedback makes it easier for me to catch and fix problems before they snowball.”[P20]
Others echoed this trust shift but stressed that increased confidence was context-dependent. Improvements were most noticeable in low-stakes, well-bounded scenarios, such as automating repetitive formatting, generating simple UI layouts, or drafting boilerplate code, where errors were easier to detect and correct. “When I first tried these tools, I wouldn’t even let them touch my main project files. Now I’ll happily use them to set up scaffolding code or handle repetitive patterns. I still double-check, but it’s way better than it used to be.”[P9] Some participants also mentioned that as AI tools became more explicit about uncertainty, they felt more comfortable using them as collaborators rather than unquestioned authorities. “It’s not that I think AI is perfect now. It’s not, but it feels more upfront about what it knows and doesn’t know. That makes me more willing to trust it with starting points and drafts, even if I’ll still do a pass myself.”[P14]
Overall, trust evolved not because participants stopped being cautious, but because they learned to pair AI’s improved capabilities with their own judgment, resulting in a more balanced, pragmatic use of the tools.
4.4.3. Theme 4.3 Security and Privacy Constraints
Security, compliance, and ethical considerations were recurring themes in determining where AI tools could be used. Participants reported actively avoiding the upload of sensitive or proprietary information into generative AI tools, including client data, internal strategy documents, and materials covered by non-disclosure agreements. “Due to compliance issues, I avoid uploading company data into generative AI tools.”[P4]
Security and privacy concerns were both technical and procedural. From a technical perspective, participants flagged risks such as prompt data leakage through cloud-based AI workflows and phishing attempts targeting teams unfamiliar with secure AI practices. One recurring issue was poor technical hygiene, such as sharing sensitive credentials in prompts. “You wouldn’t believe how many people paste API keys or production database credentials into a prompt. That’s basically handing them to whoever runs the model.”[P7] From a procedural perspective, enterprise compliance policies often restricted tool usage unless providers offered explicit contractual guarantees about data storage, retention, and non-training clauses. “Our legal team won’t let us use certain AI tools unless there’s a signed agreement on where the data goes, how long it’s stored, and a guarantee it’s not used for training.”[P12]
4.4.4. Theme 4.4 Ownership and Accountability
Participants valued AI for surfacing diverse options and accelerating execution, but across roles and domains, final responsibility was consistently positioned as a human duty. “I’m the navigator; AI is the intern.”[P11] Many tied ownership to the ideation stage rather than execution: the individual who framed the problem, set constraints, and made final calls retained the sense that the work was “theirs”, even if the AI handled much of the hands-on execution. “The work is still mine. I provide the ideas, guide AI step by step, and AI realizes them.”[P7]
At the same time, some reported feeling less connected to outputs when AI generated large portions of them, especially in tasks involving minimal manual refinement. “When AI gives me a near-finished draft and I’ve barely done anything beyond approving it, it feels like someone else’s work. I don’t feel as connected to it as I do when I’ve been hands-on from the start.”[P14] This raised concerns about the erosion of personal creative signature and the ability to stand behind the work with full confidence.
Participants widely agreed that clear attribution and traceability were essential, both to acknowledge human expertise and to identify which parts of the output could be trusted or needed review. “If you’re working with AI, you’ve got to be clear about which parts came from you and which parts came from the AI, and keep track of who signed off on what. Otherwise, it’s a nightmare to figure out who’s actually responsible or who deserves the credit.”[P17]
Copyright and IP issues were also prominent, especially in creative domains like design, writing, and illustration. Some noted unease about using AI tools trained on copyrighted works without permission, fearing both legal exposure and ethical compromises. “There’s a big legal and ethical worry about AI models trained on creative works without permission.”[P13] Some proposed adopting AI crediting guidelines similar to citation practices in academic publishing, recording prompts, documenting edits, and explicitly noting AI involvement (i.e., AI disclosure). “We should treat AI like a co-author. Just like in academic papers, you cite your sources. Here you’d record the prompts, note where AI stepped in, and document the edits. That way, anyone looking at the work later can see exactly what was human and what was AI.”[P17]
4.5. Theme 5: Human-Human and Human-AI Collaboration
Vibe coding AI tools reshaped collaboration by accelerating handoffs, reducing bottlenecks, and enabling professionals to work beyond traditional role boundaries. While it empowered individuals to take on hybrid responsibilities and act with greater autonomy, it also introduced cultural tensions, skill shifts, and organizational challenges that required adaptation in both small and large-scale team settings.
4.5.1. Theme 5.1 AI-Driven Role Shifts and Collaboration Dynamics
AI sped up human-to-human collaboration and accelerated handoffs across software engineers, designers, and product managers. “It speeds up handoff and iteration across different roles. What used to take weeks can now be done in just a few days.”[P3] Designers could directly modify code, with engineers focusing on reviewing AI-generated changes rather than writing everything from scratch. “Live, interactive prototypes inspire engineers more and make it easier to get buy-in, and prototyping is now accessible to everyone, not just designers or engineers.”[P5] UX teams often emphasized speed and results over ownership, reflecting what one participant described as a “startup mentality”[P3].
In both startups and innovation groups within larger enterprises, collaboration increasingly centered on evaluating AI outputs together, reducing dependence on technical colleagues for basic tasks. As one participant noted, “AI acts as a copilot designer or an associate PM [product manager], making communication with engineers much easier.”[P11] Compared to traditional non-AI workflows, this also reduced early-stage collaboration bottlenecks, allowing participants to progress independently before formalizing designs in shared tools. “I used to sketch something, send it to a designer, and go back and forth. It was slow. With AI, I can just do the early-stage work myself.”[P11] “In Figma, feedback from different people piles up. It’s hard to keep track and make sense of it all. With AI, I can explore ideas without that noise before I put them into shared tools.”[P15]
AI empowered professionals to work beyond traditional silos. Designers conducted research and prototyped independently, accessed backend and frontend capabilities, and expanded into marketing, operations, and competitive research. Developers reduced manual scaffolding and shifted toward product-led thinking and oversight. Participants anticipated more hybrid roles (e.g., UX engineers) and leaner teams. New skill needs emerged, including coding fluency for designers/PMs, meta-prompting, and evaluation skills, while old habits such as creating overly detailed manual mockups may be unlearned.“Designers will learn more about code, and developers will start contributing to design.”[P4]
AI also increased individual autonomy. Some reported greater flexibility, a stronger sense of ownership, and fewer interpersonal frictions, such as avoiding arguments by testing features independently. “I like the flexibility and ownership of doing both design and code. I avoid a lot of arguments.”[P1] These experiences were most common in smaller teams or among individuals already comfortable bridging design and development. By contrast, in some larger organizations, participants described a culture where ownership of design was diluted: AI outputs were treated as shared drafts that anyone could edit. “With these AI drafts, anyone can jump in and tweak things. It’s not really ‘my design’ anymore, just something we all poke at.”[P7] Senior developers described yet another pattern, where foundational expertise strengthened ownership: “Our knowledge [in coding] helped the team spot AI mistakes and steer design decisions.”[P18]
4.5.2. Theme 5.2 Junior versus Senior Roles: Job Security and Cultural Tensions
Automation of entry-level tasks raised concerns about the erosion of junior roles. Participants worried that juniors could be relegated to prompt crafting and tool operation rather than developing foundational skills. “Junior designers may end up as tool users or prompt crafters rather than concept creators. Without time and space to engage deeply with the craft, entry-level roles could be redefined in ways that erode traditional career growth.”[P4]
At the same time, senior developers increasingly took on oversight roles, relying on AI for execution while guiding overall direction. “The demand for entry-level positions is decreasing, while senior roles are shifting toward decision-making.”[P7] Generational differences also shaped adoption: younger professionals often embraced AI quickly, while some older practitioners were more hesitant. “Younger people just jump in and start using it, but older professionals can be more hesitant and risk falling behind.”[P14]
Junior designers also faced the challenge of navigating the social stigma of using vibe coding tools. Some feared being perceived as less competent, particularly by senior colleagues who were skeptical of AI-generated work. “I feel a bit ashamed using AI in front of skeptical senior engineers.”[P20] This indicated the cultural gap between early adopters and more traditional practitioners. Cultural gaps persisted between AI-generated code and developer standards. “There’s a cultural gap. Senior developers value clean, human-readable code. AI-generated code is often verbose and looks ‘junior’.”[P20] AI also introduced new social and political dynamics in the workplace. “Using AI signals efficiency to some people, but incompetence to others.”[P20]
4.5.3. Theme 5.3 Evolving Roles of AI in Human Teams
Participants framed AI not as a replacement for human collaboration, but as a reconfigurer of roles and responsibilities. Many adopted the philosophy that AI should be treated like a “junior developer”[P3], delegated to handle standardized, well-defined tasks such as generating boilerplate code, creating prototypes, or organizing files, while humans retained strategic oversight and conceptual leadership. “This frees engineers and designers to focus on higher-level judgment and decision-making, while workflows evolve to integrate AI seamlessly.”[P3]
AI was also described as an “amplifier of human intent”[P20], a thinking partner that could detect when users were stuck, teach prompt vocabulary, and explain code syntax on request. For some, this meant developing new practices of meta-coding: learning how to instruct an AI coder rather than writing every line themselves. “I stay the conceptual lead and let automation handle execution.”[P20]
Some noted AI’s educational potential, particularly for newcomers. Tools like Cursor not only generated functional code but also explained its logic, helping participants learn syntax, debug issues, and gradually acquire coding fluency. This was especially valuable for “onboarding into unfamiliar or legacy codebases”[P20]. Others saw opportunities for cross-skilling: “Designers learning more about code, and developers contributing to design.”[P4]
Participants also described their relational stance toward AI. While many treated it pragmatically as a tool, some admitted to playfully anthropomorphizing it—for example, naming agents for easier attribution “We name our AI agents so it’s easier to know which one produced what output.”[P17], saying “thank you” after prompts “I always say ‘thank you’ to it after prompts. It feels polite.”[P15], or debating whether politeness improved results “Some people say if you’re polite to AI, it gives you better results.”[P7]
Overall, participants envisioned AI evolving from an assistive tool into a more autonomous teammate, one that aligns with user intent, explains its reasoning, and integrates smoothly into workflows. Yet, participants cautioned that greater autonomy could risk overriding instead of supporting human decision-making. “Right now it’s an assistant, but I can see it becoming more like an autonomous teammate, one that understands my intent, explains its thinking, and works with me instead of just for me. It should also help me think better, not think for me.”[P10]
4.5.4. Theme 5.4 Organizational Adoption and Barriers
While participants valued the collaborative potential of vibe coding tools, large-scale adoption faced organizational hurdles. In big companies, security concerns, strict policies, and entrenched workflows made integration difficult. “Big companies have all these security concerns and policy limitations.”[P5] Existing pipelines in large organizations were often rigid, making it hard to embed new tools without disrupting established review processes. “In big organizations, vibe coding can blur responsibilities, and that can conflict with performance expectations.”[P9]
Change management and breaking long-standing professional routines were cited as critical to adoption. “The tech is there, but the challenge is changing habits.”[P8] Some pointed out that current tools lacked safeguards, such as automatic verification layers, to prevent flawed AI-generated code from reaching production. “There’s no firewall, nothing to stop bad AI code before it hits the system.”[P17] Others noted limitations in compatibility with existing design ecosystems, such as the lack of seamless integration with Figma. “It’s not yet compatible with Figma for polishing designs.”[P4]
4.6. Theme 6: Future Developments: Humans and AI
Participants reflected on the evolving human–AI relationship, identifying the skills professionals will need to learn, the habits they must unlearn, and their visions for the future of UX design with vibe coding tools. Participants pointed toward long-term reshaping of roles, tools, and creative norms.
4.6.1. Theme 6.1 Skills We Need to Learn
Participants anticipated that UX designers and engineers will increasingly shift focus from execution to higher-level tasks such as problem framing and evaluation. “It’s less about drawing and more about defining the problem and the evaluation metrics, figuring out what’s worth building in the first place.”[P6] Roles were expected to become more horizontal, with skills such as basic frontend/backend literacy supplementing traditional expertise, alongside broader fluency in product management, strategy, and marketing. “Less about pixels, more about ideas and basic code literacy.”[P5] Some participants noted that AI could already outperform them in some technical tasks, underscoring the need to shift attention toward higher-level skills. “AI can now do SQL [Structured Query Language] faster and better than me in some cases, so I focus on other skills.”[P11]
Communicating with and orchestrating AI was widely seen as a core future skill. Engineers in particular were described as “moving from code laborers to conductors, directing AI across design, debugging, and scaling tasks.”[P3] This included writing precise prompts, evaluating outputs, and coordinating workflows across different AI models. “I now need to know how to write prompts, debug AI tools, and test different models.”[P2]
Beyond technical fluency, participants stressed the importance of critical evaluation and creative restraint. Professionals must be able to judge output quality, prevent homogenization, and deliberately inject originality. “We need skills to judge code and to put art into practice to resist homogenization: creating by hand, experimenting with styles, and making deliberate choices AI would not suggest. Otherwise, everything starts to look the same.”[P4] Finally, participants noted the growing need to adapt to rapid AI tool changes. “I feel pressure to keep up with rapid AI changes. Knowing which tools to use for which tasks is a new skill to learn.”[P9]
4.6.2. Theme 6.2 Skills We Need to Unlearn
Participants emphasized the necessity of letting go of outdated workflows and rigid habits. This included pixel-perfect detailing and linear, school-taught processes such as traditional wireframing. “We need to adapt workflows to new tools, focusing more on rapid validation than traditional wireframing.”[P13] Unlearning meant moving away from time-consuming technical craftsmanship toward rapid iteration and higher-level decision-making. As P19 put it: “You’re getting out of pushing pixels. You start thinking horizontally, strategically. You are not just a maker.”[P19] This shift did not mean abandoning creativity altogether, but rather redirecting effort from low-level execution to higher-order design and strategic thinking.
Participants also highlighted the need to unlearn reliance on traditional handoff flows. Instead of lengthy designer-to-engineer exchanges, AI enables more direct iteration. “We can skip the long back-and-forth.”[P10]
4.6.3. Theme 6.3 Future of UX Design and Vibe Coding Tools
Participants envisioned a future where AI tools could translate high-level concepts into production-ready outputs with minimal manual intervention. This “magic button” ideal captured the aspiration for seamless translation from idea to execution. “If there’s one thing I want, it’s a magic button that takes my prototype and turns it into production-ready code.”[P9]
Future AI agents were imagined as proactive collaborators rather than passive executors, able to navigate large codebases, integrate UX research, and act on high-level instructions. “I hope AI can understand user dissatisfaction and generate improvements.”[P20] Some foresaw domain-specialized agents for frontend or backend tasks, synchronizing teamwork if shared interaction norms were established. “Agents can specialize by domain, like a front-end agent for UI tasks and a backend agent for server code. When these agents are used well, it helps synchronize work across team members.”[P17] Others compared future AI to ubiquitous productivity software: “It’s like Microsoft Word for coding.”[P12]
Initial fears of replacement were seen as giving way to a more collaborative mindset, where AI served as an inspiration and productivity booster. “At first, we said, ‘Wow, that’s mind-blowing. AI is replacing me.’ But over time, AI outputs started to inspire us to build on them.”[P16] “Hybrid workflows that combine AI’s speed with traditional tools like Figma”[P13] were considered the most likely future.
Participants also predicted a broader democratization of app creation, where individuals could build their own tools with AI assistance, reducing reliance on large companies. “Apps as we know them might vanish. We will be empowered to build our own apps with AI.”[P13] Yet, they stressed that the core challenge would remain: deciding what to build and why. “The bottleneck isn’t coding anymore, it’s figuring out the right problem to solve.”[P9]
Finally, participants anticipated more conversational interfaces and autonomous background agents, where people describe needs in natural language while AI manages integration, updates, and execution behind the scenes. “I think the future’s going to be more action-based. Instead of building apps the old way, you’ll just say what you want, the agents will quietly take care of everything in the background.”[P14] AI integration was expected to extend across industries and society. “It’s inevitable, not just in tech circles but everywhere.”[P19] Participants emphasized that this inevitability heightened the need to preserve user control, stressing the importance of reliable systems capable of correcting their own errors. “We need more accurate, self-correcting models.”[P20]
5. Discussion
5.1. Emerging Intention-to-UX Ecosystems across UX Organizational Practices
Vibe coding extends prior work on how AI reshapes coding and product design and development workflows (Kazemitabaar et al., 2023; Liu et al., 2023; Li et al., 2024). Whereas prior research showed that AI tools shift effort from writing code to validation and debugging, our findings suggest that vibe coding tools also reconfigure the broader workflow, shifting it from “pixel-first” to “function/problem-first”, and from “ideation” toward “validation” (Sec.4.1.2). Consistent with earlier work (Lin et al., 2023, 2020; Feng et al., 2023b; Li et al., 2024), participants highlighted how vibe coding supports rapid iteration, mitigates the “blank screen” problem, and enables conversational exchanges that accelerate idea generation. To that end, the most valuable aspects of UX work shift away from execution toward higher-level instruction and decision making, which aligns findings on AI non-use by designers (Cha and Wong, 2025). Yet, our study highlights bottlenecks in debugging and editing, where extensive human oversight remains critical, especially for non-programmers such as designers. Our findings suggest that possible solutions are ensuring future vibe coding tools provide more fine-grained controls, better support for non-coders in error correction, and stronger mechanisms to mitigate hallucinations and overconfidence in AI outputs (cf., recent OpenAI approach to not penalize uncertain LLM responses (Kalai et al., 2025)). At the organizational level, these shifts raise questions about how teams should redistribute expertise and what new roles may emerge for prompt engineering, UX design, and AI oversight (Li et al., 2024; He et al., 2024). Participants anticipated leaner teams where boundaries blur across designers, developers, and product managers, alongside new roles such as “prompt librarians” and “prompt QA (Quality Assurance) ” (Sec. 4.5.1). Furthermore, we find that adoption also faces practical barriers such as compliance, security, privacy, and integration with established workflows, which align with broader concerns about organizational impediments to AI tool uptake (Sergeyuk et al., 2025).
Our findings also point to the emergence of an “intention-to-UX ecosystem” (Sec. 4.1.1 & Sec. 4.4.4), where AI mediates between human goals and executable outputs (Meske et al., 2025). Participants described AI as an amplifier of human intent, shifting focus from pixel-perfection to functionality, orchestration, and strategic judgment. While the ease of prompting creates opportunities for code democratization, it also creates challenges since prompt engineering still remains difficult for non-experts (Zamfirescu-Pereira et al., 2023; Chang et al., 2023; Subramonyam et al., 2025). We uncover practical prompting strategies that can aid onboarding and adoption, and we encourage tool creators to integrate such guidance directly into their systems (Sec. 4.1.1). At the same time, the acceleration of prototype creation risks privileging speed, what we see as “intending the right design”, over reflective exploration of values and alternatives through “designing the right intention” (cf., (Tohidi et al., 2006)). Without this layer, design risks premature convergence and homogenization (Sec.4.2.2 & Sec. 4.3.3), a recurring risk when AI tools are blindly adopted for UX work (cf., (Li et al., 2024)).
5.2. AI Over-reliance, Trust, and Collaboration in UX Work Practices
As we enter a ‘blended‘ future of work where human and AI actions and outputs are intermixed (Constantinides et al., 2025), it comes as no surprise many are sounding the alarm that this may be the end of programming as we know it (e.g., (Welsh, 2022)). Regardless of the (eventual) truth of such sentiments, it is clear that there is a global shift toward vibe coding taking place. Not surprisingly, a major risk this carries is job replacement and worker deskilling from over-reliance on AI generated output. For the first, despite that we find that fears of job replacement (especially among junior designers (Li et al., 2024)) are reduced (Sec 4.6.3), at least current United States data suggests otherwise. Brynjolfsson et al. (2025a) found that early-career entry-level workers (ages 22–25) in occupations most exposed to GenAI (including software development) have experienced a significant employment decline of approximately 13% relative to less-exposed occupations. Second, even if junior UX professionals are shielded from unemployment risks, vibe coding introduces new barriers to collaboration. This is especially pronounced when asymmetry between skill sets is coupled with general (perceived) unreliability of AI generated code. This can distort social perceptions of the users and abusers of AI, which can include reputational harms (cf., (He et al., 2024)) and junior UX professionals having to navigate such social stigma in such work cultures (Sec 4.5.2). While there may be clear perceived benefits, the excess usage of AI tools lends itself to proneness of over-delivery, which can result in unintended features, and hinder collaboration on writing code, naturally leading to users for example declining AI generated merge requests (Spence, 2025). This leads to a worrisome development: on the one hand, experienced developers are less likely to trust AI-generated code (see Sec 4.5.2), where recent studies show accepting ¡44% of AI-generated code (Becker et al., 2025), and in parallel, we are observing a shift where stakeholders are shifting their over-reliance on expert developers toward over-relying on AI systems instead (see Sec 4.3.3).
While one can argue that this shift ultimately serves to increase access (see Sec 4.6.3) and democratizes coding, this comes at the risk of technical deskilling, responsibility gaps, and concerns over code quality (Meske et al., 2025; Horvat, 2025), especially given difficulties in controlling the AI tools to generate the right specifications and desired output (Liang et al., 2024). This raises a tangential yet critical point: not only can AI generated code create conflicts among coders (see Sec LABEL:sec:barriers), but between AI models themselves when they offer conflicting suggestions. This can create confusion and uncertainty, especially among more junior UX professionals. This naturally leads to users exhibiting a general distrust in AI-generated code, and showing preferences for independent learning (Akhoroz and Yildirim, 2025), a critical point within AI literacy to which we turn to next.
5.3. Vibe Coding Deskilling and the Promise of AI Literacy
Whether through general distrust of (contemporary) AI models or through impediments to collaboration with UX teammates, the desire to independently learn can simultaneously serve as an educational opportunity (see Sec 4.5.3). This helps professionals better learn and debug code, especially for junior individuals or those not trained to write code. This goes beyond crafting more effective prompts (cf., ‘prompt problems’ (Denny et al., 2024)), and instead more broadly contributes toward AI literacy interventions (cf., (Long and Magerko, 2020)) – especially since current conceptualizations of AI literacy still fall short in how understanding AI models can in fact help learners critically examine AI and its impact on socio-technical practices (Velander et al., 2024). Given the foregoing, we contend that it is important that any such educational benefits from vibe coding are carefully assessed, especially since it’s not always clear whether combinations of humans and AI produce better outcomes (cf., (Vaccaro et al., 2024)).
Furthermore, it becomes even more difficult to diagnose if we consider emerging evidence that humans themselves can inherit AI biases (Vicente and Matute, 2023), where uncritical reliance on AI systems (seen as cognitive extensions) can lead us to incorporate their errors into our own cognition (Naeem and Hauser, 2024). Consider Becker et al. (2025)’s findings where developers estimated that allowing AI reduced completion time by 20%, but instead found that allowing AI actually increases completion time by 19%, showing that AI tooling in fact slowed developers down. Furthermore, reliance on AI output may reduce cognitive effort and hinder the development of expertise among knowledge workers. While expert coders typically build rich mental schemas through sustained practice and effortful comprehension of complex code structures (Peitek et al., 2021; Floyd et al., 2017), such AI tools can short-circuit this process by offloading tasks that normally facilitate skill acquisition. Indeed, Kosmyna et al. (2025) found that prolonged AI reliance leads to measurable under-performance at neural, linguistic, and behavioral levels, raising concerns about long-term deskilling and diminished learning outcomes. Moreover, confidence in AI outputs has been shown to reduce critical thinking, shifting cognitive engagement away from deep reasoning toward verification and integration (Lee et al., 2025), which lies at the heart of the vibe coding promise, and to which we believe poses an ongoing risk.
5.4. Revisiting Creativity and Agency in Vibe Coding for UX Design
A recurrent finding from UX professionals was the so-called “productivity paradox” (Sec 4.3.3), where the speed gains from using AI resulted in individuals taking in more work. An obvious caveat highlighted was that this may lead to shallow creativity, with teams more quickly settling for first-draft or ’good enough’ outputs. This enhanced ability to work faster, in an agile manner (cf., (Da Silva et al., 2012)), easily lends itself to the promise of substantial productivity gains from GenAI. So what then remains of human effort, agency and creativity in this future of UX work? Earlier work by Li et al. (2024) and Cha and Wong (2025) found that human agency is a recurring theme among UX practitioners, where humans bring contextual understanding, ethical judgment, and accountability across design tasks, and prioritize maintaining control over their processes. With such qualities, they should be the ones to lead complex design-related decision-making. At the same time, there is a risk of creativity exhaustion (Li et al., 2024), which can result not only in homogenized design, but such speed gains can exert a high amount of pressure on human creators to review such output.
Nevertheless, it is worth asking to what extent this enhanced ability for GenAI-based divergent exploration of concepts (Sec 4.2.2) has merit. Bellemare-Pepin et al. (2025) found that LLMs can surpass average human performance on the Divergent Association Task (Olson et al., 2021), a psychological test designed to measure divergent thinking, as well as approach human creative writing abilities, though still behind in performance compared with highly creative people. However, even if GenAI surpasses people in such divergent thinking, UX professionals emphasized that humans still maintained ‘strategic oversight and conceptual leadership‘ in these emerging role shifts (Sec 4.5.1), echoing earlier findings that humans are and will remain the final arbiters of human-AI alignment (Li et al., 2024), within and beyond knowledge and creative work. While LLMs can certainly enhance creative output during assisted UX tasks and may lead to increased independence and human autonomy when working in UX teams (Sec 4.2.1, prolonged reliance on such tools may risk diminishing individuals’ creative performance (Kumar et al., 2025), which isa cause for concern over long-term impact on the flourishing and nurture of human creativity. It then comes as no surprise that UX professionals advocate for resisting homogenization through deep exploration (Sec 4.3.3) and stressing the importance of critical thinking and creative restraint through intentionally crafting learning moments that may foster artistic variation in UX work (Sec 4.6).
5.5. Perceived Ownership, AI Disclosures, and Responsibility in Vibe Coding UX Practice
A recurrent finding concerning perceptions of ownership and accountability in vibe coding workflows (Sec. 4.4.4) was that responsibility was consistently seen as a human duty, where ownership was tied to ideation rather than execution. Essentially, the person who intended the design was viewed as the true “owner”, even if AI executed much of the work. This is a shift from the early debates in AI art and its (harmful) impact on artists, where prompting a model does not mean the prompter is the artist (cf., (Jiang et al., 2023)). We find that while earlier concerns about authorship erosion and homogenization (Inie et al., 2023; Jiang et al., 2023; Li et al., 2024) still hold, they now manifest differently: instead of designs converging on similar aesthetics, homogenization occurs in the kinds of solutions AI tends to suggest (e.g., repeated interaction flows). Furthermore, ownership concerns also shifted: the risk was less about losing credit for ideas and more about losing control over execution, since AI often generated large parts of the work. At the same time, AI disclosure practices (Sec. LABEL:sec:_theme_5.5) remain inadequate (even if mandated by legislation; cf., (El Ali et al., 2024)). Labels such as “AI-assisted” can easily obscure the multi-step nature of collaboration; especially with agentic models executing hundreds of actions, where only some are accepted by UX professionals. When considering the speed-driven nature of collaborative UX environments, it is not unexpected hat vibe coding and role fluidity will produce uneven recognition of contributions. Here, hidden labor like expert prompting or debugging model output may get undervalued while those who merged the final version receive most of the credit. As such, it clear that attribution mechanisms (Clark et al., 2024) such as design logs or attribution frameworks (He et al., 2025) would be necessary to help surface this hidden work.
Furthermore, disclosures and ownership become even more complex when considering the evolving roles of humans and AI (Sec. 4.5.3). When models are playfully anthropomorphized, such as thanking the AI or giving it nicknames, it humanizes interaction and risks implying the system was acting with real intent. This makes it crucial that disclosures highlight human oversight and specify roles clearly so that agency is not blurred or overstated (Lawton et al., 2023). This essentially ties to the responsibility gap (Matthias, 2004), where responsibility for decision-making is distributed among multiple participants, including AI systems. This becomes critical when considering organization security and privacy (Sec. 4.4.3), for example unsafe practices such as pasting sensitive data into prompts, which can result in incidents like the Replit case where AI-generated changes reached production with minimal review (Fortune Staff, 2025). Taken together, our findings caution that with all the vibe coding exuberance and its purported benefits come real risks of (organizational) responsibility and accountability (cf., (Rakova et al., 2021)), which only increases as AI models are increasingly treated as part and parcel of UX teams.
5.6. Study Limitations and Future Work
We note that our study provides analysis and interpretation of only a snapshot in time of vibe coding practices, during a time with rapid AI development. AI tools and workflows emerge, fall out, or adapt quickly. While this may benefit from more longitudinal research, we believe that our findings on UX practices for individuals and organizations are nevertheless relevant in such snapshots, if only to help us steer uncertain futures marked by ever-more capable AI models. Second, our investigation opted for a breadth-first approach, mapping a wide range of perspectives across different organizational contexts. This necessarily came at the expense of digging deeper into the nuances of vibe coding workflows. As such, future work would benefit from building on this foundation with more in-depth studies of particular tools, workflows, or team practices. Finally, our participant sample was characterized as belonging to the UX profession, which reflects the increasingly fluid boundaries of UX practice. This spans multiple roles, including UX designers, front-end engineers, and product managers. While on the one hand such diversity enriched our data, it also complicates how a “UX professional” is defined, and whether or not an individual does or continues to self-identify with such as label. We see this ambiguity not just as a challenge but as a signal of shifting professional identities in the era of widespread LLM use and (anticipated and attempted) skill democratization. In this regard, this provides promsinfg avenue for future work to closely examine how responsibilities for intent-setting, execution, and evaluation are redistributed across and within UX roles, and how these changes reshape collaboration in blended human–AI teams (cf., (Constantinides et al., 2025)).
6. Conclusion
Our study examined how “vibe coding” is reshaping UX work by collapsing ideation, prototyping, and implementation into a conversational, intent driven workflow. Drawing on interviews with 20 UX professionals across enterprises, startups, and academia, we mapped a four-stage vibe coding workflow comprising ideation, AI generation, debugging, and review. We find that while vibe coding accelerates iteration, supports creativity, and lowers barriers to participation, UX professionals also reported challenges of code unreliability, integration, and AI over-reliance. Furthermore, We find that tensions emerge between efficiency-driven prototyping (“intending the right design”) and careful reflection (“designing the right intention”), which introduces new asymmetries in trust, responsibility, and potentially negative social perceptions within UX teams. Through the lens of responsible human-human and human-AI collaboration for AI-Assisted UX design and development, we reflect on and contribute to a deeper understanding of worker deskilling, ownership and AI disclosure, and the implications toward creativity safeguarding in this age of vibe coding.
Positionality and AI Usage Disclosure Statement
We recognize that author positionality shapes the perspectives and interpretations in this paper (Olteanu et al., 2023; Frluckaj et al., 2022). Our team comprises six researchers in ¡NORTH AMERICAN COUNTRY¿ and ¡EUROPEAN COUNTRY¿, with backgrounds in cognitive science, HCI, UX, machine learning, and data science. These diverse perspectives, spanning academic research and applied design, informed our research framing and analysis.
Data collection and analysis were conducted manually. We used Microsoft Teams to record and transcribe interviews and Google Notebook to organize the coding manual. Thematic analysis and affinity diagramming were carried out collaboratively by five researchers, ensuring reflexive, iterative engagement (Braun and Clarke, 2006; Harboe and Huang, 2015b). For writing, we used LLMs (GPT-5 and Claude Sonnet 4) to refine clarity, suggest structure, and enhance readability. All analytical decisions, codes, and interpretive claims remain with the human authors. Interpretation, reflection, and discussion were human-only. We disclose this AI usage to ensure transparency in AI-mediated scholarship in HCI (Elagroudy et al., 2024).
